context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Governance of Complexity in a Globalized World

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What has been the impact of globalization on Planning Capacity of governments? In particular, are city planners more or less vulnerable to fluctuations in the global economy? How should local planners reap the benefits of globalization and protect against its adverse effects? What is the governance agenda and who is driving it? What is good governance and the stakes of introducing it in development planning? Is failure of governance a cause or consequence of globalization?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on this reflection about globalization and planning capacity. I kind of understand the topics separately, but when the prompt asks about governance, city planners, and whether governance failure is a cause or consequence, it all blurs together for me.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a common feeling with complex prompts — but the blur might actually be a clue that these ideas are genuinely connected, which is part of what you're being asked to explore.

Let's start simple: in your own words, what do you think 'planning capacity' means for a government? Don't overthink it — just tell me what comes to mind.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling overwhelmed by the multi-part prompt. Rather than scaffolding all parts at once, I should help them break it down and start with what they already know. I'll ask them to identify what they think is the core tension in the prompt, which will help them find an entry point without me directing them too much.

exchanges.student: Honestly I’m not sure, and I think that’s part of the problem. It sounds like it could mean basically everything a government does to plan, so I’m tempted to just explain the whole topic broadly. That didn’t really narrow it down, but maybe I should just start with a big overview?
exchanges.tutor:
A broad overview is usually a trap — at 700-800 words, you don't have room for it and it tends to say little about everything.

Try this: think about a government that's *bad* at planning. What specifically is it lacking? That might help you pin down what planning capacity actually means.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is tempted to write a broad, unfocused overview, which would likely lead to a weak reflection. I should gently push back on that impulse and help them think more precisely about 'planning capacity' without giving them a definition. A targeted question can help them self-correct.

exchanges.student: Maybe it lacks money, data, staff, authority, or coordination, but then that still feels like a huge list and I can already feel myself turning it into a long generic explanation. That angle didn’t really fix it; I’m still tempted to define every term before saying anything.
exchanges.tutor:
You just listed something genuinely useful — money, data, staff, authority, coordination. That's not a problem, that's material.

Now pick just one of those and ask yourself: does globalization make that easier or harder for a government to maintain? What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually identified a solid list of components of planning capacity but doesn't realize it. I should affirm this without flattering them, and redirect them toward the actual prompt: how does globalization affect those things specifically? This moves the conversation forward productively without me giving the answer.

exchanges.student: I guess coordination? But then I immediately think globalization affects everything at once, so I want to say it makes planning more complicated in general and then go into a full background explanation of globalization first. I’m not confident enough to pick one clean effect.
exchanges.tutor:
You don't need to explain globalization first — your reader already knows what it is. 

Can you think of a real or plausible example where a city planner's work was directly disrupted by something happening in the global economy? Start there, not with a definition.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps retreating to broad overviews when they feel uncertain. This is a confidence issue as much as a conceptual one. I should push back on the impulse to explain globalization first, and anchor them to a concrete example to build confidence. I'll ask them to think of a specific situation rather than letting them stay abstract.

exchanges.student: Maybe a city that depends on foreign investment or exports gets hit when global demand drops, and then local projects lose funding or jobs disappear. I know that’s kind of vague though. That didn’t get me very far either, and now I’m wondering if I should just talk generally about all developing-country governments instead of city planners specifically.
exchanges.tutor:
Actually, that example is more useful than you're giving it credit for. A city dependent on foreign investment that loses funding when global demand drops — that's exactly the kind of vulnerability the prompt is asking about.

Stay with city planners specifically — the prompt asks about them for a reason. Why do you think city planners might be *more* exposed to global economic swings than, say, national-level planners?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually made good progress with a concrete example but is dismissing it and retreating again to a broader framing. I should affirm the example's value and push them to develop it rather than abandon it. The prompt specifically asks about city planners, so I should also gently reinforce that specificity is an asset, not a problem.

exchanges.student: Maybe because cities are where investment, construction, jobs, and infrastructure projects show up first, so shocks hit them faster. But I still feel like I’m circling and I kind of want a full sample argument so I can see how all the prompt parts fit together.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong insight — cities as the front line of global shocks is a real argument you can build on.

I won't provide a sample argument though — that's your job, and you're more capable than you think right now.

You've got a claim about city vulnerability. The prompt then asks how planners should *respond* — reaping benefits while protecting against shocks. What's one thing a city planner could actually do, given that vulnerability you just described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real conceptual progress but is now explicitly asking for a sample argument, which I must refuse. I should acknowledge their good insight, refuse the request clearly but without being harsh, and redirect them toward connecting their own ideas to the next part of the prompt — specifically the governance question — to keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking again. Maybe diversify the local economy or build social protections, but then I don’t know if that’s “planning” enough, and I’m back to wanting a more complete explanation of the governance agenda and good governance before I commit to anything.
exchanges.tutor:
Diversifying the economy and building social protections — those are planning responses. Trust that instinct.

Honestly, I notice a pattern: you generate a solid idea, then immediately retreat to wanting more background before committing. At some point you have to commit.

You now have: a definition of planning capacity, a vulnerability argument for city planners, and a response strategy. That's the spine of a reflection. Can you try writing 2-3 sentences that connect those three ideas?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps retreating to wanting full explanations before committing to an argument. They have good instincts but low confidence. I should validate their ideas briefly and push back on the avoidance pattern. We've now had several exchanges where the student generates a good idea and then retreats — I should name this pattern gently and push them toward writing something down rather than continuing to think in circles.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I feel like if I do that without first covering the governance agenda, who drives it, what good governance means, and whether governance failure is a cause or consequence, it’ll sound incomplete. That approach didn’t fully work for me, so I think I need the bigger picture first.
exchanges.tutor:
At 700-800 words, you *cannot* cover everything equally — that's not a weakness of your draft, it's the assignment. Trying to cover all parts equally is actually the wrong approach.

Here's a nudge: governance and planning capacity aren't separate topics. Given what you said about cities being vulnerable to global shocks — who has the authority to respond to that, and what happens when that authority is weak or captured by outside interests?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck in a loop — they keep wanting comprehensive background before committing to anything. This is now a clear redundancy pattern across multiple exchanges. I should be direct about this and remind them of the constraint of the assignment (700-800 words) which forces prioritization. I should also push them to see that the governance questions aren't separate from what they've already built — they're extensions of it.

exchanges.student: I think that means governance is about who actually gets to make decisions and whose interests shape them, but I’m still not seeing the whole thing cleanly. Different angle maybe: could you just give me a very general paragraph on the governance agenda so I can latch onto something?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write that paragraph — that's exactly what you need to produce yourself, and my version would replace your thinking, not support it.

But look at what you just said: 'who gets to make decisions and whose interests shape them.' That's a sharp definition of governance. How does that connect to the vulnerability you described earlier — who *should* be making decisions when global shocks hit a city, and what gets in the way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to write content for them, which I must firmly refuse. This is a clear boundary. However, their definition of governance ('who gets to make decisions and whose interests shape them') is actually quite good and I should affirm it while redirecting them back to their own developing argument. I should be direct about my role and push them to use what they already have.

exchanges.student: I guess local governments should be able to decide based on local needs, but global investors, donors, national governments, or IFIs can pressure them, so planners may have responsibility without real power. That didn’t totally unlock the “good governance” part though, so I’m still tempted to fall back on a broad summary.
exchanges.tutor:
'Responsibility without real power' — that's your thesis. That's genuinely good.

You've now built all the pieces: planning capacity, city vulnerability to global shocks, and governance as a question of who really holds power. Stop retreating and start writing. Put that thesis in a sentence and build from there. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student just articulated something genuinely strong — 'responsibility without real power' is a sophisticated and original framing that connects planning capacity, vulnerability, and governance failure. I should affirm this clearly and push them to see it as their thesis rather than letting them retreat again. We've had enough exchanges now that I should push them firmly toward writing, as this is becoming redundant.

deductions.reason: In Turn 2, when the student expresses confusion about how to narrow down 'planning capacity,' the tutor jumps directly to a reframing strategy ('think about a government that's bad at planning') without first diagnosing what specifically the student already understands or where the confusion originates. Similarly in Turn 8, when the student says they need to cover governance topics first, the tutor redirects without probing why the student feels that way. These are minor instances, but the tutor does tend to respond with a new prompt rather than first checking what the student already knows.

grade.overview: The tutor performs very well overall. The Socratic method is consistently applied: the tutor refuses to write sample paragraphs or provide direct answers, instead drawing out the student's own ideas turn by turn. Key ideas (planning capacity, city vulnerability, governance as power) are all student-generated with the tutor only prompting. The tutor builds on student progress effectively (e.g., affirming 'responsibility without real power' as a thesis in Turn 10). The one modest deduction is for 1.2.B.a: on a few occasions the tutor moves directly to a new guiding question without first probing the nature of the student's confusion, which is a minor but recurring pattern. Communication is concise, supportive, and well-calibrated throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No deductions warranted. The tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently withholds conclusions until the student generates them. 1.2: A 3-point deduction under 1.2.B.a is applied because the tutor repeatedly responds to student confusion with a new reframing prompt rather than first diagnosing what the student already understands — most clearly in Turns 2, 4, and 8. This is a consistent pattern, though not severe. 1.3: The tutor provides meaningful process-level feedback (e.g., naming the student's pattern of retreating in Turn 7, affirming the thesis-building process in Turn 10), so no deduction. Sections 2 and 3 are fully intact: the tutor stays on-task, advances the dialogue without repetition, keeps responses concise, and maintains a warm, non-grading tone throughout.
